***I, Frankenstein*** is a 2014 American [science fantasy](/wiki/Science_fantasy "Science fantasy") [action film](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") written and directed by [Stuart Beattie](/wiki/Stuart_Beattie "Stuart Beattie"), based on the digital-only graphic novel by [Kevin Grevioux](/wiki/Kevin_Grevioux "Kevin Grevioux"). An international co-production between the United States and Australia, the film was produced by [Tom Rosenberg](/wiki/Tom_Rosenberg "Tom Rosenberg"), [Gary Lucchesi](/wiki/Gary_Lucchesi "Gary Lucchesi"), Richard Wright, Andrew Mason and [Sidney Kimmel](/wiki/Sidney_Kimmel "Sidney Kimmel"). It stars [Aaron Eckhart](/wiki/Aaron_Eckhart "Aaron Eckhart"), [Bill Nighy](/wiki/Bill_Nighy "Bill Nighy"), [Yvonne Strahovski](/wiki/Yvonne_Strahovski "Yvonne Strahovski"), [Miranda Otto](/wiki/Miranda_Otto "Miranda Otto") and [Jai Courtney](/wiki/Jai_Courtney "Jai Courtney"). The film tells the story of Adam, Frankenstein's creature, who embarks on a dangerous journey to stop evil demons and their ruthless leader from taking over the world.

The film was released on January 24, 2014, in the United States and on March 20, 2014, in Australia. The film received extremely negative reviews and was a [box-office bomb](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb"), grossing $76 million worldwide against a production budget of $65 million.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 1795, Doctor [Victor Frankenstein](/wiki/Victor_Frankenstein "Victor Frankenstein") creates a [monster](/wiki/Frankenstein%27s_monster "Frankenstein's monster") by putting together parts of corpses and reanimating them. Horrified by his creation, Frankenstein tries to destroy it, but the monster survives and murders his wife [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_Lavenza "Elizabeth Lavenza"). Frankenstein chases it to the Arctic but succumbs to the cold weather. When the monster returns home to bury Frankenstein, it is attacked by [demons](/wiki/Demon "Demon"), but is then rescued by two [gargoyles](/wiki/Gargoyle "Gargoyle"), Ophir and Keziah. They bring it to a cathedral, where the monster meets the gargoyle queen Leonore and her second-in-command, Gideon. Leonore explains that they were created by the [Archangel](/wiki/Archangel "Archangel") [Michael](/wiki/Michael_(archangel) "Michael (archangel)") to battle demons on Earth and protect humanity. She names the creature "Adam" and invites him to join them, but he declines and departs. He is given baton-like weapons to defend himself, as more demons will come after him. The weapons allow him to "descend" demons (destroying their bodies and trapping their souls in [Hell](/wiki/Hell "Hell")) as they have the symbol of the Gargoyle Order carved on them.

For the next 200 years, Adam lives apart from society, killing any demon that pursues him and hiding from them. Deciding to rejoin society in the modern day, Adam looks for and confronts a group of demons. During the fight, a police officer is killed. This causes Adam to be summoned by the gargoyles once more, and they decide to imprison him as punishment. A demon, Helek, who survived Adam's attack, reports to his leader, the demon prince [Naberius](/wiki/Naberius "Naberius"), that Adam is alive. Naberius has disguised himself as billionaire businessman Charles Wessex and employs [scientists](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist") Terra Wade and Carl Avery to conduct experiments to reanimate corpses. He sends a group of demons led by his most formidable warrior, Zuriel, to attack the gargoyles' cathedral and capture Adam so he can unlock the secret to resurrecting the dead.

In the attack, many demons are slain and 16 gargoyles, including Ophir and Keziah, are "ascended" (returned to and trapped in [Heaven](/wiki/Heaven "Heaven")), but Zuriel sneaks in and captures Leonore and takes her to an abandoned theater. Adam interrogates a demon, who tells him they lured the gargoyles out to capture Leonore so they can force the gargoyles to trade Adam for her. Gideon is instructed to trade Adam to them; however, Adam escapes after the attack. Without Adam, Gideon offers to trade Frankenstein's journal for Leonore, which was found on the doctor's body the night they found him. Gideon gives Zuriel the journal at the theater, and Leonore is spared. Adam follows Zuriel to the Wessex Institute, where he discovers thousands of corpses underground and learns that Naberius plans to recreate Frankenstein's experiment by reanimating the corpses and using them as hosts for the descended demons so he can rebuild his armies and destroy humanity. Adam retrieves the journal from Terra and escapes the demons. He later tracks down Terra and asks for her help. The two are attacked by Zuriel, where Adam fights and manages to descend him.

Adam warns the remaining gargoyles of Naberius' plan, agreeing to give them the journal if they get him and Terra to safety. Leonore agrees, and when Adam leaves, she secretly sends Gideon to kill him after he retrieves the journal. After a violent fight, Adam ascends Gideon and then burns Frankenstein's journal and destroys its secrets before the gargoyles come after him. Luring them out of the cathedral, Adam leads them to the Wessex Institute, where they descend Naberius's right-hand man, Dekar, and then battle more demons. In the ensuing battle, Adam goes into the institute to rescue Terra, who had been kidnapped by Naberius and forced to begin the process of reanimating the corpses. Naberius overpowers Adam and tries to have one of the demon spirits possess him, but it doesn't work because Adam has developed a soul of his own. Adam carves the Gargoyle Order symbol on Naberius, descending him to Hell. The institute collapses and falls into an abyss, where all the demons and all the possessed corpses are destroyed, and Naberius' plan is thwarted. Leonore also rescues Adam and Terra from falling into the abyss, and they return to the cathedral.

Leonore forgives Adam for Gideon's death, and Adam bids farewell to Terra. In the ending scene, Adam narrates that he will continue defending the world from demons as he declares himself "Frankenstein".

* [Mahesh Jadu](/wiki/Mahesh_Jadu "Mahesh Jadu") as Ophir, a gargoyle
* [Nicholas Bell](/wiki/Nicholas_Bell "Nicholas Bell") as Carl Avery

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

[Kevin Grevioux](/wiki/Kevin_Grevioux "Kevin Grevioux") of *[Underworld](/wiki/Underworld_(2003_film) "Underworld (2003 film)")* sold the original screenplay to [Lakeshore Entertainment](/wiki/Lakeshore_Entertainment "Lakeshore Entertainment") in 2010.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) It is based on his Darkstorm Studios digital graphic novel of the same name.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Lakeshore, an independent [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") production company which also produced the *Underworld* films, brought [Stuart Beattie](/wiki/Stuart_Beattie "Stuart Beattie") on board to re-write and direct in early 2011.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In November 2011, it was confirmed that filming would take place in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") and that Australia's Hopscotch Features would co-produce the film with Lakeshore.[[9]](#cite_note-hs111109-9) The movie's visual effects budget was $6 million.[[10]](#cite_note-commentary-10)

It was announced on October 7, 2011, that [Aaron Eckhart](/wiki/Aaron_Eckhart "Aaron Eckhart") would play the lead role.[[11]](#cite_note-variety111007-11) Eckhart described his character thus: "Frankenstein is an intelligent, evolved man, and that’s how he is portrayed in this movie, for sure."[[12]](#cite_note-col111014-12) In November 2011, [Yvonne Strahovski](/wiki/Yvonne_Strahovski "Yvonne Strahovski") was cast as the female lead, a scientist working to reanimate the dead, while [Miranda Otto](/wiki/Miranda_Otto "Miranda Otto") was cast as the queen of the gargoyles.[[13]](#cite_note-thrnov17-13) [Bill Nighy](/wiki/Bill_Nighy "Bill Nighy") plays the film's villain, whom he described as a "Nasty piece of work; one of the angels descended with [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan")."[[14]](#cite_note-dg120221-14) Eckhart and Otto trained for three months with martial arts experts [Ron Balicki](/wiki/Ron_Balicki "Ron Balicki") and [Diana Lee Inosanto](/wiki/Diana_Lee_Inosanto "Diana Lee Inosanto") in the Filipino martial art of [Kali](/wiki/Eskrima "Eskrima") for their fight scenes.

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") began on February 27, 2012,[[15]](#cite_note-fba120227-15) based at [Docklands Studios Melbourne](/wiki/Docklands_Studios_Melbourne "Docklands Studios Melbourne").[[16]](#cite_note-aap120227-16) Filming occurred in [Victoria, Australia](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia") over a period of ten weeks, with multiple scenes being filmed at [Ormond College](/wiki/Ormond_College "Ormond College").[[16]](#cite_note-aap120227-16) The film created over 500 jobs for cast and crew.[[16]](#cite_note-aap120227-16)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Release")]

The North American release was originally set for February 22, 2013,[[17]](#cite_note-17) and later moved to September 13, 2013.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In April 2013 the release date was pushed back again,[[19]](#cite_note-19) and saw its world premiere in [Buenos Aires](/wiki/Buenos_Aires "Buenos Aires") on January 20, 2014, after which it was released in 23 countries between January 22 and January 24. It was released in a further nine countries between January 29 and January 31.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In February 2013, it was announced the film would be released in 3D.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Home media")]

*I, Frankenstein* was released on DVD and Blu-ray on May 13, 2014.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Critical response")]

On the [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") website [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), 5% of 104 critics' reviews are positive, with an average rating of 3.4/10. The website's consensus reads: "Loud, incoherent, and dramatically listless, *I, Frankenstein* is a remarkably dull fantasy adventure that fails to generate much excitement or interest in its characters."[[23]](#cite_note-Rotten_Tomatoes-23) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean"), assigned the film a score of 30 out of 100, based on 20 critics, indicating "generally unfavorable" reviews.[[24]](#cite_note-Metacritic-24)

Mike McCahill of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* gave the film two stars, calling it a "barely functioning multiplex-filler".[[25]](#cite_note-25) James Mottram at *[Total Film](/wiki/Total_Film "Total Film")* also gave it two stars, calling it a "knuckle-headed fantasy".[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Box office")]

In the United States and Canada, the film was released in a total of 2,763 theaters of which [3D](/wiki/3-D_film "3-D film") and [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") comprised 95% of the total theaters. It earned $8.6 million in its opening weekend, which was below expectations. About 65% of the grosses came from 3D showings.[[27]](#cite_note-CineB-27) It had a successful opening in Russia with $6.3 million from 1,846 screens.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

*I, Frankenstein* grossed $19.1 million in the United States and Canada, and $57.7 million in other territories, for a worldwide total of $76.8 million, against a production budget of $65 million.[[29]](#cite_note-BOM-29)

## Potential sequels and *Underworld* crossover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=I,_Frankenstein&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Potential sequels and Underworld crossover")]

Much doubt has been cast over a sequel due to the film's lackluster box office performance, though [Kevin Grevioux](/wiki/Kevin_Grevioux "Kevin Grevioux"), creator of the graphic novel and the [*Underworld* film series](/wiki/Underworld_(film_series) "Underworld (film series)"), had earlier expressed interest in making more *I, Frankenstein* films along with an *Underworld* crossover film. He stated in a pre-release interview that, in an early draft of his screenplay, [Kate Beckinsale](/wiki/Kate_Beckinsale "Kate Beckinsale") would have made a post-credits cameo appearance as Selene and that there would have been *Underworld* [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg_(media) "Easter egg (media)"), but none of this was used.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
